title: Chip-carving knife technique
subtitle: The three-cut sequence for geometric chip carving
excerpt: The foundational three-cut chip sequence for geometric chip carving. Three converging cuts lift a triangular chip from the surface. The starting technique for decorating spoon handles, coasters, and flat panels.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for chip-carving patterns and the three-cut method. Early-twentieth-century art-school woodwork manuals. The Mechanic's Workshop Companion on decorative carving.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content (8 to 12 percent for indoor use).
A leather strap charged with abrasive compound, used to maintain a sharp edge between sharpenings.

content.text: Chip carving removes small triangular chips from a flat surface to build up a geometric pattern. Three cuts converge at a common point and lift the chip cleanly. The pattern grows from repeating this sequence across a grid. The chip-carving knife has a wide flat blade ground to a 65-degree point; it is held with the thumb on the spine to push through the cut.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
When to use it
Chip carving suits flat surfaces on
seasoned

timber: spoon handles, wooden coasters, small panel inserts in frames, trinket-box lids. It works best on lime, sycamore, and cherry; the close grain holds crisp chip edges. Avoid open-grained woods like oak or ash for fine chip work; the grain breaks out.
What you need
A chip-carving knife and a
strop

. The edge must be sharp enough to shave hair cleanly. A dull edge drags rather than cuts and the chip tears rather than lifting as a single piece. Strop before every practice session and every fifteen minutes during carving.
The three-cut sequence
Mark a triangle on the wood surface. A simple equilateral triangle 15 mm on a side is a good starting size. The three lines of the triangle mark the three cuts you will make.

First cut: place the knife at one corner of the triangle, blade angled at 65 degrees to the wood surface. Press the thumb on the spine and push the blade along one side of the triangle to the opposite corner. The blade travels angled inward so that when it reaches the corner, it is at the correct depth to meet the chip point.
Second cut: pivot the workpiece 120 degrees. Make the same push cut along the second side of the triangle from the same starting corner, angled inward at 65 degrees.
Third cut: pivot 120 degrees again. Make the third cut along the third side. If the three cuts have met cleanly at the central low point, the chip lifts out as a single piece.
What a correct chip looks like

The chip comes out in one piece, with a smooth concave triangular floor. The edges of the chip depression are crisp and clean. If the chip crumbles or the floor is ragged, the edge is dull or the cuts did not meet at the same depth. Strop and practise on a scrap piece before returning to the main work.
Common mistakes

Blade not angled inward on each cut: the three cuts meet at the surface rather than at a low point, and the chip does not lift. Each cut must angle toward the centre of the triangle, not straight down.
Three cuts at different depths: the chip breaks rather than lifting cleanly. Consistent blade angle on each cut is what makes the three cuts meet at the same point.
Cutting against the grain on the triangle sides: the edge of the depression tears out rather than cutting clean. Rotate the workpiece so each cut travels with the grain.
